codehover — a complete showcase

codehover

What is codehover?

codehover turns a plot built up step by step into an interactive code table. You hover a row of code and the figure beside it changes to show the plot as it looks up to that line. It is a great way to teach how a ggplot2 chart is assembled, one + at a time.

There are two ways in:

  • ch_hover({ ... }) — the automatic API. Give it ggplot code; it splits at every top-level +, renders one image per step and returns a finished, self-contained hover table (CSS and JavaScript travel with it).
  • ch_int() / ch_row() / ch_out() — the manual API, for full control over the rows and the images (any images at all, not only + chains).

This page demonstrates every feature using only built-in datasets (palmerpenguins) and Brazilian maps from geobr. Hover the code rows below!

1. The hero: ch_hover() incremental

The default mode is incremental: hovering a row highlights it and every prior row, and the figure shows the cumulative result up to that step. Let’s build a good-looking penguins scatter plot from scratch.

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = .8) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "turbo", end = .85) +
    labs(
      title = "Bill dimensions of Palmer penguins",
      subtitle = "Hover each line of code to see that step",
      x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Bill depth (mm)", color = NULL
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")
}, width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
scale_color_viridis_d(option = "turbo", end = 0.85) +
labs(title = "Bill dimensions of Palmer penguins", subtitle = "Hover each line of code to see that step",
x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Bill depth (mm)", color = NULL) +
theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
theme(legend.position = "top")

Start at the top row — just the ggplot() call draws an empty panel — and walk down: the points appear, get a colour palette, gain titles, then a polished theme.

2. Comparing viridis palettes

A fun use of the cumulative behaviour: stack several colour scales. Each new scale_color_viridis_c() replaces the previous one (ggplot keeps only the last scale of each type), so hovering down the rows walks you through the viridis palettes on the very same plot.

First build a complete base plot and store it in g:

g <- ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g,
                          color = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  labs(title = "Penguin size, coloured by body mass",
       x = "Flipper length (mm)", y = "Body mass (g)", color = "Mass (g)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Now hover through the palettes:

ch_hover({
  g +
    scale_color_viridis_c() +
    scale_color_viridis_c(option = "turbo") +
    scale_color_viridis_c(option = "magma") +
    scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
    scale_color_viridis_c(option = "cividis")
}, width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
g +
scale_color_viridis_c() +
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "turbo") +
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "magma") +
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "cividis")

Note. Because the scales are added, the code shown is cumulative (+ viridis + turbo + magma + ...). Visually only the last scale on each row wins, which is exactly what makes the palette-by-palette reveal work. When you want each row to be a genuinely independent plot instead, use the manual API (see Section 9).

3. Fixed scales and layout

fixed_scales

By default every step shows the true output of its partial code, so axes can jump as layers change the data range. With fixed_scales = TRUE the axes, legends and panel layout are pinned from the final plot, giving a stable reveal (mechanism borrowed from the ggreveal package).

Default (axes may move between steps):

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
    geom_point(alpha = .5) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "firebrick") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(170, 235))
}, width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "firebrick") +
scale_x_continuous(limits = c(170, 235))

Same code with fixed_scales = TRUE (axes pinned from the start):

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
    geom_point(alpha = .5) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "firebrick") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(170, 235))
}, fixed_scales = TRUE, width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "firebrick") +
scale_x_continuous(limits = c(170, 235))

layout

layout controls where the figure sits relative to the code table:

  • "auto" (default) — side by side when there is room, image wraps below for wide code tables.
  • "row" — always side by side; the image shrinks to fit.
  • "column" — always below the code.

Forced row:

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(species, body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
    geom_boxplot() +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako", end = .85) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}, layout = "row", width = 5, height = 3.2, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(species, body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
geom_boxplot() +
scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako", end = 0.85) +
theme(legend.position = "none")

Forced column:

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(species, body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
    geom_boxplot() +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako", end = .85) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}, layout = "column", width = 5, height = 3.2, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(species, body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
geom_boxplot() +
scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako", end = 0.85) +
theme(legend.position = "none")

4. type = "one_row"

The other hover effect highlights only the hovered row instead of the row and all the rows above it. The figure still shows the cumulative result; only the highlight differs. Compare hovering here with Section 1.

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm, color = island)) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = .8) +
    labs(x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Flipper length (mm)", color = NULL)
}, type = "one_row", width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(bill_length_mm, flipper_length_mm, color = island)) +
geom_point(size = 2.5) +
scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = 0.8) +
labs(x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Flipper length (mm)", color = NULL)

5. Maps with geobr

ch_hover() works with anything that prints as a plot, including sf maps. Here we build a choropleth of the five Brazilian regions, step by step.

states <- read_state(year = 2020, showProgress = FALSE)
ch_hover({
  ggplot(states) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = name_region), color = "white", linewidth = .1) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
    labs(title = "Brazil by region", fill = "Region") +
    theme_void(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}, width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 72)
ggplot(states) +
geom_sf(aes(fill = name_region), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
labs(title = "Brazil by region", fill = "Region") +
theme_void(base_size = 12) +
theme(legend.position = "bottom")

geobr caches its downloads, so the first render needs a network connection but later renders are offline.

6. Custom styling with css_class

Pass css_class = "yourclass" and define that class in a <style> block to restyle the table — the hover highlight colour, the font, borders, anything. All codehover rules are scoped under .codehover, so target .yourclass tr.hover, .yourclass table, and so on.

.fancy table {
  font-family: "Fira Code", "Cascadia Code", monospace;
  font-size: 0.85em;
}
.fancy td {
  border-bottom: 1px solid #eee;
}
.fancy tr.hover {
  background-color: #2c3e50;   /* dark slate instead of the default yellow */
  color: #ffffff;
}
ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
    geom_density(alpha = .6, color = NA) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "rocket", end = .85) +
    labs(x = "Body mass (g)", y = "Density", fill = NULL)
}, css_class = "fancy", width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
geom_density(alpha = 0.6, color = NA) +
scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "rocket", end = 0.85) +
labs(x = "Body mass (g)", y = "Density", fill = NULL)

For one-off tweaks you can also push inline attributes straight onto the tags via table_tag_add / div_tag_add in the manual API, e.g. ch_int(table_tag_add = "style='background:#fafafa'").

7. Keep the source clean with ch_hover_chunk()

In a report you may not want the plot code written twice. Put it in a normal chunk with eval=FALSE and reference it by label with ch_hover_chunk(), which reads the chunk’s code and feeds it to ch_hover().

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, fill = sex)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "cividis", end = .7, na.value = "grey80") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
  coord_flip()

Then a single inline call renders the hover table:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, fill = sex)) +
geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "cividis", end = .7, na.value = "grey80") +
labs(x = NULL, y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
coord_flip()

8. Keep the HTML small with path =

By default each step image is embedded in the HTML as base64, so the page is fully self-contained but can get large. Pass a folder to path = and codehover writes numbered PNGs to disk and references them by relative path instead — smaller HTML, but the folder must ship alongside the page.

ch_hover({
  ggplot(penguins, aes(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)) +
    geom_hex(bins = 18) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno") +
    labs(x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Bill depth (mm)", fill = "Count")
}, path = "showcase_assets/", width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
ggplot(penguins, aes(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)) +
geom_hex(bins = 18) +
scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno") +
labs(x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Bill depth (mm)", fill = "Count")

After rendering, look in showcase_assets/ — you’ll find codehover-1.png, codehover-2.png, … one per step.

9. The manual API

When your steps are not a single ggplot + chain — independent plots, arbitrary images, data-wrangling stages — build the table by hand with ch_int() → ch_row() → ch_out(). This is also the correct way to compare palettes when you want each row to be a genuinely separate plot (contrast with Section 2).

Render each palette to its own PNG first:

base <- ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, color = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  labs(x = "Flipper length (mm)", y = "Body mass (g)", color = "Mass (g)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

palettes <- c("viridis", "turbo", "magma", "plasma", "cividis")

files <- vapply(palettes, function(opt) {
  f <- tempfile(fileext = ".png")
  ggsave(f, base + scale_color_viridis_c(option = opt),
         width = 6, height = 3.8, dpi = 72)
  f
}, character(1))

Now assemble the table — each row is an independent image, and the code shown is whatever text you choose:

tbl <- ch_int(type = "one_row")
for (i in seq_along(palettes)) {
  tbl <- ch_row(tbl,
                text = paste0("scale_color_viridis_c(option = \"", palettes[i], "\")"),
                img  = files[i])
}
ch_out(tbl, img = files[1])
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "viridis")
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "turbo")
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "magma")
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma")
scale_color_viridis_c(option = "cividis")

Here hovering each row shows only that palette — no cumulative stacking, because every row points at its own pre-rendered image.

Recap

Feature How
Incremental reveal ch_hover({ ... }) (default)
Highlight one row ch_hover(..., type = "one_row")
Stable axes across steps ch_hover(..., fixed_scales = TRUE)
Image placement ch_hover(..., layout = "auto" / "row" / "column")
Custom styling ch_hover(..., css_class = "...") + a <style> block
Clean source ch_hover_chunk("label") on an eval=FALSE chunk
Smaller HTML ch_hover(..., path = "folder/")
Full control / any images ch_int() → ch_row() → ch_out()
